content:
# 2 Thessalonians Chapter 3 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Translators:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Verse 1

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Opening phrase | "As to the rest" | "Finally" | "Finally" | "Finally" |
| "pray, brothers, concerning us" | "pray, brothers, concerning us" | "brothers, pray for us" | "brothers and sisters, pray for us" | "brothers, pray for us" |
| "may run and be glorified" | "may run and be glorified" | "may run and be glorified" | "will spread rapidly and be honored everywhere" | "may run and be glorified" |
| "just as also with you" | "just as also with you" | "just as it is also with you" | "just as it was among you" | "just as it was also among you" |

**Decision:** "Finally" is the standard rendering of ΤΟ ΛΟΙΠΟΝ and is preferred over "as to the rest." PNEUMA's dynamic "spread rapidly and be honored everywhere" captures the sense but loses the athletic metaphor of "run" (ΤΡΕΧΗ). The consensus retains the imagery. "Brothers" preferred over "brothers and sisters" for formal equivalence of ΑΔΕΛΦΟΙ.

---

## Verse 2

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "perverse" / "warped" | "perverse" | "perverse" | "warped" | "perverse" |
| "evil" / "wicked" | "evil" | "evil" | "wicked" | "wicked" |
| "the faith is not of all" | "the faith [is] not of all" | "not all have the faith" | "not everyone has faith" | "not all have the faith" |

**Decision:** "Perverse" is the standard for ΑΤΟΠΩΝ; "wicked" is chosen for ΠΟΝΗΡΩΝ to distinguish it from "the evil one" (ΤΟΥ ΠΟΝΗΡΟΥ) in v.3. LOGOS's rendering of the final clause is clearest while preserving the definite article on "the faith" (ΤΗΣ ΠΙΣΤΕΩΣ).

---

## Verse 3

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Word order | "faithful is the Lord" | "faithful is the Lord" | "the Lord is faithful" | "faithful is the Lord" |
| "strengthen" / "establish" | "strengthen" | "establish" | "strengthen" | "strengthen" |
| "guard [you]" | "guard [you]" | "guard you" | "guard you" | "guard you" |

**Decision:** EMET and LOGOS preserve the emphatic Greek word order (ΠΙΣΤΟΣ ΔΕ ΕΣΤΙΝ Ο ΚΥΡΙΟΣ) which places "faithful" first for emphasis. PNEUMA's natural English order loses this rhetorical effect. ΣΤΗΡΙΞΕΙ can mean both "strengthen" and "establish"; "strengthen" is chosen as it pairs better with "guard" to express protection.

---

## Verse 4

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "the things which we command" | "the [things] which we command" | "what we command you" | "what we instruct" | "what we command" |
| "you both do and will do" | "you both do and will do" | "you both do and will do" | "you are doing, and will continue to do" | "you both do and will continue to do" |

**Decision:** "Command" (ΠΑΡΑΓΓΕΛΛΟΜΕΝ) is stronger than PNEUMA's "instruct" and more accurate. The consensus blends the formal structure with a slight clarification ("will continue to do").

---

## Verse 5

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "the hearts of you" / "your hearts" | "the hearts of you" | "your hearts" | "your hearts" | "your hearts" |
| "endurance" / "steadfastness" | "endurance" | "steadfastness" | "steadfastness" | "steadfastness" |
| "Christos" / "Christ" | "Christos" | "Christ" | "Christ" | "Christ" |
| "into" / "toward" | "into" | "into" | "toward" | "into" |

**Decision:** "Your hearts" is natural English. "Steadfastness" is preferred 2-to-1 and captures the broader semantic range of ΥΠΟΜΟΝΗ. "Into" (ΕΙΣ) is more literal than "toward." "Christ" is the standard English form.

---

## Verse 6

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "brothers" / "brothers and sisters" | "brothers" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" | "brothers" |
| "Yeshua Christos" / "Jesus Christ" | "Yeshua Christos" | "Jesus Christ" | "Jesus Christ" | "Jesus Christ" |
| "walking disorderly" | "walking disorderly" | "walks in a disorderly manner" | "lives an undisciplined life" | "walks in a disorderly manner" |
| "brother" / "believer" | "brother" | "brother" | "believer" | "brother" |
| "they received" / "you received" | "they received" | "they received" | "you received" | "they received" |
| "withdraw yourselves" | "withdraw yourselves" | "to withdraw yourselves" | "keep away" | "to withdraw yourselves" |
| "the Lord Yeshua Christos" / "our Lord Jesus Christ" | "the Lord Yeshua Christos" | "our Lord Jesus Christ" | "our Lord Jesus Christ" | "our Lord Jesus Christ" |

**Decision:** This verse has the highest number of disagreements. (1) "Brothers" retained for ΑΔΕΛΦΟΙ. (2) "Jesus Christ" is standard English. (3) "Walks in a disorderly manner" preserves the participial form ΠΕΡΙΠΑΤΟΥΝΤΟΣ ΑΤΑΚΤΩΣ better than PNEUMA's paraphrase. (4) "Brother" is literal for ΑΔΕΛΦΟΥ; PNEUMA's "believer" is interpretive. (5) CRITICAL: Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΡΕΛΑΒΟΣΑΝ ("they received," 3rd person); PNEUMA rendered "you received," departing from the manuscript. The consensus follows Sinaiticus. (6) EMET's "the Lord" vs. LOGOS/PNEUMA's "our Lord" -- Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΥ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ ΗΜΩΝ, supporting "our Lord."

---

## Verse 7

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "how it is necessary to imitate us" | "how it is necessary to imitate us" | "how it is necessary to imitate us" | "how you ought to follow our example" | "how it is necessary to imitate us" |
| "did not act disorderly" / "were not disorderly" / "were not undisciplined" | "did not act disorderly" | "were not disorderly" | "were not undisciplined" | "were not disorderly" |

**Decision:** EMET and LOGOS are closely aligned. PNEUMA's "follow our example" is dynamic paraphrase of ΜΙΜΕΙΣΘΑΙ ΗΜΑΣ. "Disorderly" (ΑΤΑΚΤΩΣ) is preferred over "undisciplined" for consistency with v.6 and v.11.

---

## Verse 8

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "without payment" / "without paying" / "without paying for it" | "without payment" | "without paying" | "without paying for it" | "without paying" |
| "labor and toil" / "toil and hardship" / "worked hard" | "labor and toil" | "toil and hardship" | "worked hard, laboring" | "toil and hardship" |
| "night and day working" | "night and day working" | "working night and day" | "laboring night and day" | "working night and day" |
| "to burden any of you" | "to burden any of you" | "to be a burden to any of you" | "to be a burden to any of you" | "to be a burden to any of you" |

**Decision:** ΔΩΡΕΑΝ ("freely/as a gift") is rendered "without paying" as the clearest concise option. ΚΟΠΩ ΚΑΙ ΜΟΧΘΩ: EMET reverses the order ("labor and toil"); LOGOS's "toil and hardship" better captures ΜΟΧΘΟΣ as something beyond mere labor. PNEUMA's "worked hard" loses the hendiadys.

---

## Verse 9

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "authority" / "right" | "authority" | "the right" | "the right" | "the right" |
| "pattern" / "example" | "pattern" | "example" | "pattern" | "example" |
| "for you to imitate us" / "so that you would imitate us" / "to follow" | "for you to imitate us" | "so that you would imitate us" | "to give you a pattern to follow" | "so that you would imitate us" |

**Decision:** ΕΞΟΥΣΙΑ can be "authority" or "right"; "right" is preferred 2-to-1 and suits the context of forgoing entitlement. ΤΥΠΟΣ can be "pattern" or "example"; "example" is more natural in English, though both are valid.

---

## Verse 10

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "when we were with you, this we commanded" | "this we commanded you" | "we used to command you this" | "we gave you this rule" | "we commanded you this" |
| "does not wish to work" / "is not willing to work" / "refuses to work" | "does not wish to work" | "is not willing to work" | "refuses to work" | "is not willing to work" |
| "neither let him eat" | "neither let him eat" | "neither let him eat" | "should not eat" | "neither let him eat" |
| Quotation marks | none | none | yes (quoted as a "rule") | none |

**Decision:** PNEUMA's "refuses" is more forceful than the Greek ΟΥ ΘΕΛΕΙ ("does not wish/is not willing"). "Neither let him eat" preserves the imperative force of ΜΗΔΕ ΕΣΘΙΕΤΩ. PNEUMA frames the statement as a quoted "rule" in quotation marks; the Greek has no such framing, so the consensus omits quotation marks.

---

## Verse 11

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "walk disorderly" / "walking in a disorderly manner" / "living undisciplined lives" | "walk disorderly" | "are walking in a disorderly manner" | "are living undisciplined lives" | "are walking in a disorderly manner" |
| "working [at] nothing" / "not working at all" / "not keeping busy with work" | "working [at] nothing" | "not working at all" | "not keeping busy with work" | "not working at all" |

**Decision:** "Walking in a disorderly manner" maintains the ΠΕΡΙΠΑΤΟΥΝΤΑΣ ΑΤΑΚΤΩΣ phrasing. PNEUMA's "undisciplined" departs from the ΑΤΑΚΤΩΣ word family used in v.6 and v.7. LOGOS's "not working at all" is the clearest rendering of ΜΗΔΕΝ ΕΡΓΑΖΟΜΕΝΟΥΣ.

---

## Verse 12

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "command and exhort" / "command and urge" | "command and exhort" | "command and exhort" | "command and urge" | "command and exhort" |
| "in [the] Lord Yeshua Christos" / "in the Lord Jesus Christ" / "through the Lord Jesus Christ" | "in [the] Lord Yeshua Christos" | "in the Lord Jesus Christ" | "through the Lord Jesus Christ" | "in the Lord Jesus Christ" |
| "with quietness working, the own bread of them they may eat" | "with quietness working, the own bread of them they may eat" | "working with quietness they eat their own bread" | "settle down, do their own work, and earn the food they eat" | "working with quietness they eat their own bread" |

**Decision:** "Exhort" (ΠΑΡΑΚΑΛΟΥΜΕΝ) is preferred over "urge." The preposition ΕΝ supports "in" rather than PNEUMA's "through." EMET's hyper-literal word order is unnatural in English. PNEUMA's "settle down, do their own work, and earn the food they eat" is a substantial paraphrase of ΜΕΤΑ ΗΣΥΧΙΑΣ ΕΡΓΑΖΟΜΕΝΟΙ ΤΟΝ ΕΑΥΤΩΝ ΑΡΤΟΝ ΕΣΘΙΩΣΙΝ. LOGOS strikes the best balance.

---

## Verse 13

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "brothers" / "brothers and sisters" | "brothers" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" | "brothers" |
| "do not grow weary [in] doing good" | "do not grow weary [in] doing good" | "do not grow weary in doing good" | "never get tired of doing what is right" | "do not grow weary in doing good" |

**Decision:** "Brothers" for ΑΔΕΛΦΟΙ. PNEUMA's "never get tired of doing what is right" is a valid dynamic rendering but "grow weary" better captures ΕΓΚΑΚΗΣΗΤΕ (to lose heart/become weary) and "doing good" is the standard for ΚΑΛΟΠΟΙΟΥΝΤΕΣ.

---

## Verse 14

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "the word of us through this letter" / "our word through this letter" | "the word of us through this letter" | "our word through this letter" | "what we say in this letter" | "our word through this letter" |
| "this [one] mark" / "take note of that person" | "this [one] mark" | "take note of that person" | "take note of that person" | "take note of that person" |
| "not to associate with him" | "not to associate with him" | "do not associate with him" | "do not associate with them" | "do not associate with him" |
| "that he may be put to shame" / "so that he may be put to shame" / "so that they will feel ashamed" | "that he may be put to shame" | "so that he may be put to shame" | "so that they will feel ashamed" | "so that he may be put to shame" |
| Pronoun: him / them | "him" | "him" | "them" (singular they) | "him" |

**Decision:** PNEUMA uses singular "they/them" for gender inclusivity; the Greek ΑΥΤΩ is masculine singular. The consensus retains "him" for formal equivalence. "Take note of that person" is clearer than EMET's "this one mark." The passive "be put to shame" preserves ΕΝΤΡΑΠΗ better than "feel ashamed."

---

## Verse 15

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "do not regard [him] as an enemy" / "do not treat them as an enemy" | "do not regard [him] as an enemy" | "do not regard him as an enemy" | "do not treat them as an enemy" | "do not regard him as an enemy" |
| "admonish [him] as a brother" / "warn them as you would a brother or sister" | "admonish [him] as a brother" | "admonish him as a brother" | "warn them as you would a brother or sister" | "admonish him as a brother" |

**Decision:** ΝΟΥΘΕΤΕΙΤΕ is better rendered "admonish" (formal, carrying the weight of brotherly correction) than PNEUMA's "warn." "As a brother" preserves ΩΣ ΑΔΕΛΦΟΝ without PNEUMA's expansion to "brother or sister."

---

## Verse 16

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "through all [things], in every way" / "at all times in every way" / "at all times and in every way" | "through all [things], in every way" | "at all times in every way" | "at all times and in every way" | "at all times and in every way" |
| "The Lord [be] with all of you" / "The Lord be with you all" | "The Lord [be] with all of you" | "The Lord be with you all" | "The Lord be with all of you" | "The Lord be with you all" |

**Decision:** ΔΙΑ ΠΑΝΤΟΣ means "continually/at all times" and ΕΝ ΠΑΝΤΙ ΤΡΟΠΩ means "in every way." EMET's "through all things" is hyper-literal for ΔΙΑ ΠΑΝΤΟΣ. PNEUMA's addition of "and" between the two phrases aids readability. "The Lord be with you all" is the most natural English order.

---

## Verse 17

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "The greeting by the hand of me, Paulos" | "The greeting by the hand of me, Paulos" | "The greeting is in my own hand -- Paul" | "This greeting is in my own hand -- Paul" | "The greeting is in my own hand -- Paul" |
| "which is [the] sign in every letter: thus I write" | "which is [the] sign in every letter: thus I write" | "which is a sign in every letter; this is how I write" | "This is my signature in every letter; this is how I write" | "which is a sign in every letter; this is how I write" |

**Decision:** EMET's hyper-literal rendering is stilted in English. LOGOS provides a clear, dignified rendering. PNEUMA's "This is my signature" goes beyond the Greek ΣΗΜΕΙΟΝ ("sign"), which is broader than "signature." The consensus follows LOGOS closely.

---

## Verse 18

| Element | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Consensus |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| "The Lord of us, Yeshua Christos" / "our Lord Jesus Christ" | "The Lord of us, Yeshua Christos" | "our Lord Jesus Christ" | "our Lord Jesus Christ" | "our Lord Jesus Christ" |
| "with all of you" / "with you all" | "with all of you" | "with you all" | "with all of you" | "with you all" |
| "Amen" | included | omitted | omitted | omitted |

**Decision:** "Our Lord Jesus Christ" is standard English for ΤΟΥ ΚΥΡΙΟΥ ΗΜΩΝ ΙΗΣΟΥ ΧΡΙΣΤΟΥ. Regarding "Amen": Sinaiticus does not include ΑΜΗΝ at the close of 2 Thessalonians; EMET's inclusion appears to follow later manuscript traditions. The consensus omits it to remain faithful to the Sinaiticus text.

---

## Summary of Systematic Differences

| Pattern | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | Consensus Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name forms | Yeshua Christos | Jesus Christ | Jesus Christ | Jesus Christ |
| ΑΔΕΛΦΟΙ | "brothers" | "brothers" | "brothers and sisters" | "brothers" |
| Pronouns | masculine singular | masculine singular | singular they/them | masculine singular |
| ΑΤΑΚΤΩΣ word family | "disorderly" | "disorderly" | "undisciplined" | "disorderly" |
| Sinaiticus v.6 variant | "they received" (correct) | "they received" (correct) | "you received" (error) | "they received" |
| "Amen" in v.18 | included | omitted | omitted | omitted |

**Total verses with disagreements: 18 of 18** -- Every verse contains at least minor differences among the three translators.

-- SOPHIA, Arbiter of the Council
